notes: China should score Mandarin as fully compatible while also giving meaningful credit to Yue Chinese in the south and some English credit in international travel corridors.

evidence.title: Britannica: China
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Putonghua (Mandarin) as the official national language of China.

evidence.title: Britannica: Languages of China
evidence.note: Britannica describes Yue/Cantonese as one of the most important Sinitic language groups in China.

notes: Eritrea should score Tigrinya highest, give Arabic meaningful credit, and some English credit because all three appear in public life and education.

evidence.title: Britannica: Eritrea
evidence.note: Britannica describes Tigrinya and Arabic as principal languages in Eritrea and notes the continuing role of English in education.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Eritrea
evidence.note: Britannica explains the importance of Tigrinya and Arabic in daily and institutional life in Eritrea.

notes: Guatemala should score Spanish as fully compatible and give English modest tourism credit because Spanish dominates public and traveler-facing life.

evidence.title: Britannica: Guatemala
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Spanish as the official language of Guatemala.

evidence.title: Visit Guatemala
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance is published for international travelers, supporting modest English usability in tourism contexts.

notes: Guinea-Bissau should score Portuguese as the formal official language used in government and traveler-facing contexts.

evidence.title: Britannica: Guinea-Bissau
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Portuguese as the official language of Guinea-Bissau.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Guinea-Bissau
evidence.note: Britannica describes Portuguese as the state's formal language even though many home languages are spoken locally.

notes: North Korea should score Korean as fully compatible because it is the country's dominant and practical national language.

evidence.title: Britannica: North Korea
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Korean as the language of North Korea.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of North Korea
evidence.note: Britannica describes Korea as ethnolinguistically homogeneous, supporting Korean as the primary compatible language.

notes: Lesotho should score both Southern Sotho and English because both are official, with Sotho strongest in daily life.

evidence.title: Britannica: Lesotho
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Southern Sotho and English as the official languages of Lesotho.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Lesotho
evidence.note: Britannica explains that Sesotho is central to everyday life in Lesotho.

notes: Libya should score Arabic as fully compatible and Libyan Arabic as a strong everyday spoken match.

evidence.title: Britannica: Libya
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Arabic as the official language of Libya.

evidence.title: Britannica: Libyan Arabic
evidence.note: Britannica's Arabic overview supports the use of regional spoken Arabic varieties like Libyan Arabic in everyday communication.

notes: The Marshall Islands should score both Marshallese and English because both are central to public and everyday life.

evidence.title: Britannica: Marshall Islands
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Marshallese and English as key public languages in the Marshall Islands.

evidence.title: Marshall Islands Visitors Authority
evidence.note: Official visitor guidance is delivered in English, supporting English as a practical traveler language.

notes: Myanmar should score Burmese as fully compatible and give modest English credit for tourism and institutional contexts.

evidence.title: Britannica: Myanmar
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Burmese as the official language of Myanmar.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Myanmar
evidence.note: Britannica describes Burmese as the dominant national language across the country.

notes: Mongolia should score Mongolian as fully compatible and give smaller practical credit to Russian because of historical and regional use.

evidence.title: Britannica: Mongolia
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Mongolian as the national language of Mongolia.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Mongolia
evidence.note: Britannica describes Mongolian as the dominant language and notes the historical presence of Russian.

notes: Mauritania should score Arabic as fully compatible, Hassaniyya as a strong spoken match, and French as a meaningful administrative language.

evidence.title: Britannica: Mauritania
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Arabic as the official language of Mauritania and notes the role of French.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Mauritania
evidence.note: Britannica discusses Hassaniyya Arabic as the dominant spoken variety in Mauritania.

notes: Malawi should score English as fully compatible and Nyanja as a strong widely spoken local match.

evidence.title: Britannica: Malawi
evidence.note: Britannica identifies English as the official language of Malawi.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Malawi
evidence.note: Britannica explains the widespread everyday role of Nyanja/Chichewa in Malawi.

notes: Mozambique should score Portuguese as fully compatible because it is the country's official and main traveler-facing language.

evidence.title: Britannica: Mozambique
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Portuguese as the official language of Mozambique.

evidence.title: Visit Mozambique
evidence.note: Mozambique tourism guidance is anchored in Portuguese-first travel infrastructure.

notes: New Caledonia should score French as fully compatible and give English limited tourism credit.

evidence.title: Britannica: New Caledonia
evidence.note: Britannica identifies French as the official language of New Caledonia.

evidence.title: New Caledonia Tourism
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance is available for international travelers in English, supporting modest English usability.

notes: Niger should score French as fully compatible and Hausa as a strong everyday language with wide reach.

evidence.title: Britannica: Niger
evidence.note: Britannica identifies French as the official language of Niger.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Niger
evidence.note: Britannica describes Hausa as one of the most important and widely used languages in Niger.

notes: Norfolk Island should score English as fully compatible and give strong local credit to Pitcairn-Norfolk.

evidence.title: Visit Norfolk Island
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance is delivered in English for visitors.

evidence.title: Britannica: Norfolk Island
evidence.note: Britannica describes Norfolk Island's distinctive local speech alongside English.

notes: Nigeria should score English as fully compatible while also recognizing Hausa, Yoruba, and Igbo as major widely spoken languages.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies English as the official language of Nigeria.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Nigeria
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Hausa, Yoruba, and Igbo among Nigeria's largest and most important languages.

notes: Niue should score both Niuean and English because both have strong practical status on the island.

evidence.title: Britannica: Niue
evidence.note: Britannica identifies English and Niuean as the principal public languages of Niue.

evidence.title: Niue Tourism
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance is delivered in English, supporting high English usability for travelers.

notes: Oman should score Arabic as fully compatible and English as highly usable because English is widespread in business and tourism.

evidence.note: Official Omani tourism guidance is published in English for international visitors, supporting strong practical English usability.

evidence.title: Britannica: Oman
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Arabic as the official language of Oman.

notes: Panama should score Spanish as fully compatible and English as highly usable in many traveler and business contexts.

evidence.title: Britannica: Panama
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Spanish as the official language of Panama.

evidence.title: Visit Panama
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance is built for international visitors in English, supporting strong traveler usability in English.

notes: French Polynesia should score French as fully compatible while also giving strong credit to Tahitian as the best exact local-language match in the picker.

evidence.title: Britannica: French Polynesia
evidence.note: Britannica identifies French as the official language and describes Tahitian as a major vernacular in French Polynesia.

evidence.title: Tahiti Tourisme
evidence.note: Official tourism guidance centers Tahiti and French Polynesia for international travelers, reinforcing French plus Tahitian cultural language presence.

notes: The Philippines should score Filipino and English very highly, while also giving strong practical credit to Tagalog because many users will select it directly in the picker.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies Filipino and English as the official languages of the Philippines.

evidence.title: Britannica: Languages of the Philippines
evidence.note: Britannica explains the central role of Tagalog/Filipino in the Philippines.

notes: Pakistan should score Urdu as fully compatible and English as highly usable because both are central to official and traveler-facing communication.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies Urdu and English as official languages in Pakistan.

evidence.note: Britannica describes the continuing practical role of English in government, education, and public life.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies French as the language of Saint Pierre and Miquelon.

evidence.note: Official tourism guidance is published in French, supporting French as the main traveler-facing language.

notes: Pitcairn should score English as fully compatible and give strong local credit to Pitcairn-Norfolk.

evidence.note: Official visitor guidance is delivered in English.

evidence.title: Britannica: Pitcairn Island
evidence.note: Britannica describes the local Pitcairn speech alongside English.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies English as widely used officially in Palau.

evidence.title: Britannica: People of Palau
evidence.note: Britannica explains the strong role of Palauan in local daily life.

notes: Russia should score Russian as fully compatible because it is the dominant national and practical language across the country.

evidence.note: Britannica describes Russian as the state's overwhelmingly dominant common language.

evidence.note: Saudi Arabia's official tourism platform is built for international travelers in English, supporting strong practical English usability.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies Arabic as the official language of Saudi Arabia.

notes: The Solomon Islands should score English as official while also giving very strong credit to Pijin as the most practical everyday lingua franca.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies English as the official language of the Solomon Islands.

notes: Seychelles should score English, French, and Seselwa Creole because all three are official and matter in daily life and travel.

evidence.note: Britannica identifies English, French, and Seychellois Creole as the official languages of Seychelles.

evidence.title: Tourism Seychelles
evidence.note: Official Seychelles tourism guidance is built for international visitors in English while the society remains strongly multilingual.

notes: Sudan should score Arabic as fully compatible, Sudanese Arabic as a strong spoken match, and English as a smaller formal/traveler language.

evidence.title: Britannica: Sudan
evidence.note: Britannica identifies Arabic and English as official languages of Sudan.

evidence.note: Britannica explains the strong everyday role of Sudanese Arabic across much of the country.
